meta.title: Field Experience from the Edge
meta.subtitle: How builders are adapting AI in real work

throughLines.title: The bottleneck moved upstream
throughLines.summary: Execution got cheaper. Taste, judgment, QA, and production readiness became the scarce work.

throughLines.title: Developer identity is changing
throughLines.summary: From hand-coding to orchestrating agents, reviewing output, and building CLI surfaces agents can use.

throughLines.title: Context is the unsolved layer
throughLines.summary: Shared memory, scoped context, and atomized private agent loops threaten coordination.

throughLines.title: Verification everywhere
throughLines.summary: Frontend QA, oral exams, PR review, and coworker-not-replacement support agents.

encounters.hook: Personal CRM, doer/reviewer agentic coding, and the risk that private agent loops thin shared context.

highlights.text: Agentic coding works better as a doer/reviewer pipeline with deterministic pre-push checks.
A self-hosted personal CRM ingests messages and transcripts without handing full history to third parties.
More work mediated by personal agents could unbundle project work from the relationships that make collaboration meaningful.
Open source may get stronger in the AI era when bug-finding is paired with repair and verification loops.

posts.title: Agentic Coding Works Better as a Doer/Reviewer Pipeline

encounters.bio: RaidGuild veteran and DAOhaus alum building a self-hosted personal CRM. Daily driver: Claude Code with doer/reviewer agent loops.

encounters.hook: Proxy collapse in education, AI as patient tutor, and the shift from execution scarcity to taste and judgment.

highlights.text: Reflection papers used to proxy for understanding — AI makes those proxies unreliable.
Oral exams and interview-style assessment move evaluation closer to live explanation.
When execution gets cheap, taste, sequencing, and distribution get expensive.
Model orchestration and context management remain daily pain points even for power users.

posts.title: When Execution Gets Cheap, Taste Gets Expensive
Proxy Collapse Came For The Reflection Paper

encounters.bio: UVA McIntire professor teaching applied AI and entrepreneurship. Running live experiments on assessment after proxy collapse.

encounters.hook: Engineering did not end — it moved up the stack. Friction still matters.

highlights.text: Agents change product and engineering work without removing the need for judgment at the edges.
Without friction, there is no moat — some resistance still shapes durable work.
The grind of hand-coding feels less central, but specification and review gain leverage.

posts.title: Engineering Did Not End. It Moved Up the Stack.
Without Friction, There Is No Moat

encounters.bio: Engineer and builder thinking about agents, product friction, and where judgment still lives in the stack.

encounters.hook: The hard part of agent orchestration is shared state — not the prompt.

highlights.text: Multi-agent workflows break when state and handoffs are implicit.
Coordination harnesses need observable checkpoints humans can inspect.
Local agent loops are powerful but can hide process from the team.

posts.title: The Hard Part Of Agent Orchestration Is Shared State

encounters.bio: RaidGuild builder working on agent orchestration, shared state, and coordination harnesses for multi-agent work.

encounters.hook: When production gets cheap, the premium is the human edit.

highlights.text: AI-assisted production still needs a human gate for taste and final cut.
Speed without editorial judgment produces volume, not signal.

posts.title: The Premium Is the Human Edit

encounters.bio: Creative producer focused on editorial craft — the human edit as premium when AI accelerates production.

encounters.hook: AI can shorten alignment, but it cannot choose the taste.

highlights.text: Community tools may need to matchmake contributors, not just host chat.
Alignment work compresses with AI; aesthetic and values choices do not.

posts.title: AI Can Shorten Alignment, But It Cannot Choose The Taste

encounters.bio: Community and alignment builder exploring taste, contributor matchmaking, and tools beyond chat rooms.

encounters.hook: The new senior dev job is reviewing the machines — building CLIs and wrappers agents can run.

highlights.text: Trust in AI coding built gradually; early output needed as much verification as manual work.
The middle of a project is easier now; edges like deploy, infra, and governance remain human-heavy.
Small command surfaces and internal CLIs become high-leverage agent interfaces.

posts.title: The Middle Is Easy Now. The Edges Are The Work.

encounters.bio: Experienced developer moving from hand-coding toward orchestrating AI coworkers and CLI surfaces agents can run.

encounters.hook: Context transfer is the new agency bottleneck — teams still copy-paste between agent sessions.

highlights.text: Custom software gets cheaper; medium businesses may own more of their stack.
Codex-heavy workflows work when scope is clear; frontend QA still needs human review.
Scoped context sharing between clients, projects, and teammates remains unsolved.
Some clients now open PRs on internal tools that the consultancy reviews.

posts.title: Context Transfer Is The New Agency Bottleneck

encounters.bio: Founder of Datalist Consulting — custom software, self-hosting, and Codex-heavy workflows for operational clients.

encounters.hook: The first useful AI support agent is a coworker, not a replacement.

highlights.text: Reviewed automation in support workflows beats magic all-purpose agents.
Domain-specific tooling and human checkpoints matter more than generic prompts.

posts.title: Reviewed AI Support Agents Start As Coworkers

encounters.bio: Builder of reviewed support workflows — useful AI agents start as coworkers with human checkpoints.

encounters.hook: Product bottlenecks are moving — speed and ambition rose, but testing and should-we decisions got harder.

highlights.text: Small teams can ship overnight what used to take a week — bottlenecks shift to QA and judgment.
Public goods and builder-relations work still needs distribution and production readiness.
Maybe yes we could, but should we? becomes the recurring product question.

encounters.bio: Product and builder-relations work around public goods, Octant, and Flow State. Claude Code in the terminal.

encounters.hook: Agent readiness starts with cleanup — not magic prompts.

highlights.text: Repos and docs need hygiene before agents can operate reliably.
Realistic automation gates beat aspirational full-agent replacement.

posts.title: Agent Readiness Starts With Cleanup, Not Magic

encounters.bio: Operator focused on repo hygiene, cleanup, and realistic gates before agents can do reliable work.

encounters.hook: After the prototype, judgment does the work — PRDs compress into testable builds fast.

highlights.text: Brain dump → PRD scaffold → agent prompts → prototype is the new product loop.
AI can bloat products unless someone keeps scope simple and the experience delightful.
Bullish on democratized creation; cautious about hype cycles and AGI timelines.

posts.title: After the Prototype, Judgment Does the Work

encounters.bio: Product lead with Protocol Labs background — brain dump to PRD to prototype is the new loop.

encounters.hook: The first useful ad ops agent is a QA auditor — domain judgment beats generic automation.

highlights.text: Programmatic ad workflows need audited checks, not blind generation.
Domain-specific QA agents outperform all-purpose copilots in operations work.

posts.title: The First Useful Ad Ops Agent Is A QA Auditor

encounters.bio: Ad ops practitioner building domain QA agents — audited checks beat blind generation in programmatic workflows.

encounters.hook: Creative labor after LLMs — synthesis and taste over raw drafting, with agent-first design and stopping rules.

highlights.text: Writer value shifted to rumination, research, and synthesis — not competing on raw drafting volume.
Individual AI productivity is often wasted without organizational source-of-truth and scoped data access.
Agent-first experiences and trusted personal agents may mediate how people interact with other systems.
When everything is possible, the production skill is knowing when to stop — scope creep and token maxing dilute the work.
Built the Fuzzy novel digital companion on OneReach.ai — an agentic reader experience, not just a static ebook.

posts.title: The Hard Part Of AI Production Is Stopping

encounters.bio: UX Magazine editor and co-author of Age of Invisible Machines. Rebuilt the magazine with Cursor and Claude; built the Fuzzy novel companion on the OneReach.ai platform.
